zones.name: Crash Recovery Subsystem
zones.description: The web viewer's crash detection and graceful recovery subsystem, comprising a crash-detector utility and a React hook that restores viewer state after unhandled errors.
insights: Low cohesion (0.29) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone
High coupling (0.71) — 3 imports target "web-dashboard"

Coupling of 0.71 exceeds the warning threshold but reflects a deliberate design: crash-recovery hooks back into web-dashboard viewer state and web-dashboard wires the crash-detector at the composition root — both directions are intentional.
The zone's two production files (crash-detector.ts, use-crash-recovery.ts) have matching dedicated tests, confirming the subsystem is well-tested relative to its size.
The small, focused scope (2 production files, 3 test files) is a positive signal — crash handling is isolated from the rest of the viewer rather than scattered across components.

Coupling 0.71 exceeds the 0.6 warning threshold; this is an artifact of the bidirectional composition-root wiring with web-dashboard documented in zone hints rather than uncontrolled dependency spread.

Cohesion 0.29 is below the 0.4 threshold, driven by the small file count and the fact that crash-detector.ts and use-crash-recovery.ts have different primary importers; this is acceptable for a two-file subsystem.
Isolating crash detection into its own zone prevents error-handling concerns from leaking into viewer component logic — a clean separation of the unhappy path.

The combination of low cohesion (0.29) and high coupling (0.71) in a 5-file zone is a structural signal that this zone may be too small to stand alone — its two production files have different primary importers within web-dashboard, weakening internal cohesion while maximizing external coupling.

crash-detector.ts and use-crash-recovery.ts serve complementary but distinct roles (detection vs. recovery state), which is the root cause of the low cohesion metric; they are coupled to each other only indirectly through web-dashboard's composition root.

The crash-recovery zone exhibits the 'forced satellite' anti-pattern: two files that don't directly import each other are grouped together because they share a thematic concern (crash handling) rather than an import relationship. The low cohesion (0.29) is a direct consequence. If this zone were dissolved and both files pinned to web-dashboard, the cohesion metric would disappear but architectural clarity would improve.

crash-recovery ↔ web-dashboard bidirectional coupling (3 imports each direction) creates a small import cycle. Unlike the mcp-route-layer, which has a type-only reverse edge, crash-recovery's reverse imports are runtime (use-crash-recovery.ts hooks back into viewer state). This makes crash-recovery the only zone in this batch with a true runtime circular dependency with web-dashboard, even if intentional.

crash-detector.ts and use-crash-recovery.ts have no direct import edge between them — all coupling passes through web-dashboard as an intermediary. This means the zone's internal cohesion will never improve without restructuring how crash detection signals flow to recovery state, because the two files are only loosely 'crash-related' rather than architecturally coupled to each other

The three-file test suite (crash-detector.test.ts, use-crash-recovery.test.ts, crash-detector-test-support.ts) is larger by file count than the two-file production surface it covers; the shared test helper (crash-detector-test-support.ts) implicitly documents a cross-file dependency that does not exist in production code, further reducing cohesion signal reliability

The runtime bidirectional dependency between crash-recovery and web-dashboard (3 imports each direction) has no interface boundary — crash-recovery directly imports from web-dashboard viewer state and web-dashboard directly imports crash-detector. If crash handling ever needs to support server-side error boundaries (process signals, middleware), the viewer-coupled implementation cannot be reused without extracting a shared interface, and the coupling surface would have to expand into a new zone simultaneously.

use-crash-recovery.ts is a Preact framework hook (imports from preact/hooks, manages component state) while crash-detector.ts is framework-agnostic and uses only browser platform APIs (sessionStorage). Grouping a framework-coupled hook with a framework-free platform utility conflates the Preact component layer with the browser platform layer — crash-detector.ts is portable to any framework or server-side context but its placement alongside a Preact hook obscures this portability.

Zone name 'crash-recovery' lacks the 'web-' prefix used by peer web-package zones (web-dashboard, web-landing-scripts, web-static-assets), creating the same package-prefix filtering gap as mcp-route-layer.

crash-detector.ts uses only sessionStorage and has zero framework dependencies — it could be reused in a Node.js or alternative-framework context without modification. Separating it from the Preact-coupled use-crash-recovery.ts (e.g. pinning crash-detector.ts to a platform-utilities zone) would make its portability explicit and prevent it from acquiring accidental Preact coupling via zone co-location.
Rename zone from 'crash-recovery' to 'web-crash-recovery' to align with the 'web-' prefix convention and enable reliable package-level zone filtering.

llm-provider-core is the hub in a hub-and-spoke topology: both llm-runtime-support (23 imports) and llm-provider-tests (25 imports) depend on it, but neither depends on each other — core is the sole integration point.

The 3 downward imports from core into support suggest utilities were logically promoted to core status but physically left in support — the fix is to either move those 3 utilities up into src/ or extract them into a shared foundation file rather than reaching down.

llm-provider-core acts as a hub with two independent spoke zones (support and tests); this is a sound layered pattern as long as the hub does not import from its spokes — the 3 reverse imports into support break this invariant.

The interface between llm-provider-core and llm-provider-tests is clean and one-directional (25 test imports, 0 reverse) — this is a well-defined boundary and should be preserved as a model for the support layer.

config.ts is the sole origin of all 3 bidirectional edges: api-provider.ts, cli-provider.ts, and create-client.ts each import only from config.ts in the support zone — moving config.ts to the core zone would eliminate the cycle in a single file move with no other changes required.

create-client.ts calls resolveApiKey from config.ts directly rather than accepting a resolved key as a parameter — the factory hard-codes a config resolution side-effect, making unit testing without filesystem fixtures impossible and coupling the factory to the config layer.

codex-cli-provider.ts resolves configuration inline (resolveCodexModel, codexConfig parameter) without using the shared config.ts utilities, while cli-provider.ts delegates to resolveCliPath — two CLI providers use divergent config resolution strategies within the same zone.

create-client.ts imports resolveApiKey from config.ts (support zone) rather than accepting a pre-resolved key — factory functions should receive resolved values via parameters, not perform I/O side-effects internally. This couples the factory to the filesystem and makes isolated unit testing require real config fixtures.

codex-cli-provider.ts and cli-provider.ts implement divergent config resolution strategies: cli-provider.ts delegates to resolveCliPath from config.ts while codex-cli-provider.ts resolves config inline via a passed codexConfig parameter. The inconsistency means the two CLI provider implementations cannot be substituted interchangeably and will diverge further as config logic evolves.

createLLMClient() in llm-client.ts returns ClaudeClient rather than LLMProvider — the top-level vendor-neutral factory bypasses the generic provider interface it was designed to unify, so callers typed to LLMProvider cannot use createLLMClient() without a cast.

Duplicate vendor dispatch exists across provider-registry.ts and llm-client.ts: both contain independent if-else branches on the 'codex' vendor name that must be kept in sync when new vendors are added. provider-registry.ts is the designated extensibility point, but llm-client.ts maintains a parallel hard-coded dispatch that circumvents the registry.

suggest.ts and help-format.ts export pure CLI presentation utilities (Levenshtein distance, color formatting) with no imports from any provider or LLM-related module, yet they are first-class exports of the foundation package. They are in the core zone by file placement but have no conceptual relationship to LLM provider infrastructure.

createLLMClient() return type is ClaudeClient instead of LLMProvider — change the return type annotation so the vendor-neutral factory returns the generic interface. createClient() already returns ClaudeClient & LLMProvider, so only the llm-client.ts wrapper needs updating.

llm-client.ts hardcodes a codex branch independently of ProviderRegistry — callers adding a third vendor must update both files. Remove the manual dispatch from llm-client.ts and delegate to defaultRegistry.getActiveProvider() to make the registry the single extension point.

suggest.ts and help-format.ts are CLI presentation utilities with no LLM logic — their presence in the public.ts barrel inflates the package's surface with non-LLM concerns. Consider grouping them under a documented 'CLI utilities' section in public.ts or extracting to a subpath export (e.g. @n-dx/llm-client/cli) to signal the different role.

AuthMode (types.ts) and ProviderAuthMode (provider-interface.ts) are duplicate auth-mode types — AuthMode should be replaced with ProviderAuthMode or aliased to it so ClaudeClient.mode and ProviderInfo.mode share the same type. The current mismatch makes ClaudeClient structurally incompatible with LLMProvider even for equivalent values.

codex-cli-provider.ts hardcodes DEFAULT_CODEX_MODEL without a config.ts counterpart — add a resolveCodexModel utility to config.ts parallel to resolveModel() for Claude to establish a single authoritative default-model resolution path per vendor.

The OPENAI_API_KEY retry-without-key workaround in codex-cli-provider.ts embeds an undocumented environmental assumption about Codex CLI credential precedence — document this behavior in a JSDoc block or inline comment at the point where CodexConfig is defined in llm-types.ts so downstream consumers know the env interaction exists.

Two zero-cohesion residual zones (web-unit, logo) remain unresolved and both span package boundaries; applying zone pins to route each file to its canonical zone should be the next sourcevision maintenance action.

The bidirectional import cycles between web-dashboard and crash-recovery (3 imports each direction) and between web-dashboard and MCP route layer are both documented as intentional composition-root patterns; they should be annotated in zone hints to suppress false coupling warnings in future runs.

The web-dashboard zone's 367-file size dwarfs all other zones in this batch; if future analysis detects cohesion degradation, splitting viewer UI from server infrastructure into sub-zones would be a natural refactoring boundary.

The bidirectional web↔web-viewer import cycle (13 + 9 = 22 crossing imports) is the largest coupling surface in the graph; it is architecturally intentional but warrants periodic auditing to ensure no new non-gateway imports are added outside the documented composition-root pattern.

The crash↔web-viewer bidirectional dependency (3 imports each direction) suggests the crash recovery subsystem is tightly integrated with the viewer rendering layer; if crash recovery ever needs to be extracted or reused outside the web package, this coupling would need to be broken.
packages/web/src/viewer/hooks/ contains files from 3 zones (crash-recovery, web-dashboard, web-viewer-utilities) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries
File "packages/hench/tests/e2e/cli-init.test.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in packages/hench/src/schema/ but lives in packages/hench/tests/e2e/ — consider relocating

web-viewer (367 files) is the sole coupling hub in the graph: it participates in bidirectional import cycles with three separate zones (web-dashboard 9+13, web-server 2+3, crash 3+3). No other zone has more than one bidirectional coupling partner — this asymmetry should be monitored as the viewer grows.

The crash zone (5 files, cohesion 0.29) directs 100% of its external coupling at web-viewer with no direct link to web-dashboard or web-server. This satellite pattern means crash-recovery cannot be extracted without first breaking the web-viewer coupling, raising the cost of any future modularization.

web-server (4 files) is the smallest zone yet participates in bidirectional coupling with web-viewer (2+3 imports). At 5 crossing imports for a 4-file zone, it has the highest coupling density per file in the graph — a fragile surface to expand.

The four zero-coupling zones (project-root, rex-runtime-data, architecture-docs, hench-agent) form a clean 'consumed but non-importing' tier. Every zone with non-zero coupling is contained within the web package, confirming that cross-package discipline is strong and the coupling problem is scoped entirely to the web package's internal zone boundaries.

The web-dashboard ↔ web-viewer bidirectional cycle (22 crossing imports) plus web-server ↔ web-viewer (5 imports) plus crash ↔ web-viewer (6 imports) totals 33 crossing imports all incident on web-viewer, creating a triangle of cycles (web ↔ viewer ↔ server, web ↔ viewer ↔ crash) that share web-viewer as their common vertex. Any change to web-viewer's public surface has three separate bidirectional ripple paths.

The web package's zone topology is a classic hub-and-spoke with web-dashboard at the center. Three satellite zones (mcp-route-layer, crash-recovery, and the two residual zones) all couple into web-dashboard but have zero coupling to each other — they are independent spokes with no lateral dependencies. This is a clean pattern for satellite zones but concentrates all change risk in the hub.

The only runtime import cycle in this batch is crash-recovery ↔ web-dashboard (3 imports each direction). mcp-route-layer's reverse edge is type-only and erased at compile time. Distinguishing these two cases is important: mcp-route-layer is a clean ACL with no runtime cycle, while crash-recovery has a genuine bidirectional runtime dependency that should be monitored for expansion.

The two residual zones (web-landing-scripts, web-static-assets) together account for 13 misclassified files. Resolving them via zone pins is a hygiene action with no code changes required and would reduce the false-positive noise in zone health dashboards for the entire web package.

Across this batch, the gateway pattern (domain-gateway.ts in mcp-route-layer) is the only mechanism preventing direct imports from web-dashboard into sourcevision. If the gateway were bypassed, the coupling score for web-dashboard would spike and the anti-corruption layer would collapse. Automated enforcement via domain-isolation.test.js is the correct safeguard.

The crash zone's 3 external imports all target web-viewer with no coupling to web-server — crash recovery is viewer-surfaced exclusively. If server-side crash handling (e.g. process signal trapping, middleware error boundaries) is ever needed, the coupling surface must expand beyond the viewer layer, which currently has no server-side crash path.

The web-server zone contains only 4 files yet receives 3 imports from web-viewer (75% file-count saturation) while sending 2 back — the highest coupling density per file of any zone in the graph. At this size, web-server behaves more like an interface declaration than a subsystem; any growth in responsibilities would immediately raise coupling to critical levels.

The web(19 files) → web-viewer(367 files) direction carries 13 imports while web-viewer → web carries 9 — the smaller zone imports more from the larger than it receives back. This size-asymmetric bidirectional coupling suggests web-viewer is not fully encapsulated: it exports internal symbols upward to its consumer rather than exposing a stable narrow API, which is the inverse of the expected dependency direction.

The .rex and docs zones both show zero coupling and zero cohesion — they are consumed artifacts (JSON state files, markdown documentation) rather than modules, so import-graph metrics are structurally meaningless for them. Zone health tooling should skip cohesion/coupling thresholds for artifact-only zones to prevent false-positive warnings.

web-viewer is a coupling hub with three simultaneous bidirectional import cycles (web-dashboard, web-server, crash). All other zones have at most one coupling partner. This asymmetry concentrates change risk in a single 367-file zone.

Hub-and-spoke is the dominant architectural pattern for the web package in this batch. web-dashboard is the hub; mcp-route-layer, crash-recovery, and the two residual zones are spokes. No spoke-to-spoke imports exist. This topology is healthy for isolation but creates a single point of change amplification: any refactoring of web-dashboard's public surface requires coordinated updates across all satellite zones.

Test coverage discipline is uneven across zones: mcp-route-layer has 1:1 production-to-test file pairing, crash-recovery has 2 production files matched by 3 test files (including a shared helper), but the two residual zones (web-landing-scripts, web-static-assets) contain test files mixed with non-test production files — a classification artifact that inflates apparent test presence in those zones.

Size-asymmetric bidirectional coupling between web (19 files, 13 outgoing) and web-viewer (367 files, 9 outgoing): the consumer imports more symbols from the provider than the provider re-imports from the consumer. This inverts the expected encapsulation direction and indicates web-viewer's API boundary is insufficiently narrow — internal symbols are escaping rather than being mediated through a stable facade.

.rex (6 files) and docs (20 files) zones have zero cohesion and zero coupling because they contain only data artifacts and markdown — import-graph metrics cannot characterize their health. Applying zone-type annotations (e.g. 'artifact', 'documentation') and excluding them from cohesion/coupling thresholds would eliminate structural false positives in automated analysis.

All non-zero coupling in the monorepo is contained within the web package — rex, sourcevision, hench, and orchestration scripts produce zero external coupling. The web package's internal zone graph is the only coupling surface requiring ongoing governance.

The two smallest zones by file count (mcp-route-layer: 4 files, crash-recovery: 5 files) have the highest coupling scores (0.6 and 0.71 respectively). This inverse size-coupling relationship is typical of intentional boundary zones that are small by design but must bridge large architectural gaps. It is a feature, not a defect, but both zones should be monitored to ensure their coupling scores do not increase as new files are added.

The web-viewer-utilities zone's unresolved viewer file misclassification creates a compound measurement problem: the 0.67 coupling score is artificially inflated by viewer-to-web-dashboard imports, while the build infrastructure files (build.js, dev.js) contribute zero coupling. Until zone pins are applied, automated alerts triggered by the 0.67 threshold will be false positives that desensitize operators to genuine coupling violations.

There is no enforcement gap for cross-package gateway discipline (covered by domain-isolation.test.js) but there is an enforcement gap for intra-package layering within large zones like hench-agent (155 files). The project has strong outer-boundary governance and weak inner-boundary governance — a pattern that typically leads to zone cohesion decay over time as a large zone accumulates shortcuts between its own subdirectories.

The three bidirectional import cycles in the web package (web-dashboard↔web-viewer, web-viewer↔web-server, web-viewer↔crash) all share web-viewer as their common vertex and are documented as intentional. However, there is no automated check that prevents a fourth bidirectional cycle from being added to web-viewer accidentally. A coupling-cap lint rule (e.g. 'web-viewer must not acquire new bidirectional cycle partners') would keep the intentional-coupling surface stable.

The MCP gateway split (rex-gateway in web-dashboard, domain-gateway in mcp-route-layer) creates a hidden cross-zone governance gap: automated gateway audits that scan mcp-route-layer will miss the rex cross-package surface entirely, and audits that scan web-dashboard will find gateway logic mixed with composition-root wiring rather than isolated in an ACL zone.

Four of the five zones in this batch contain misclassified files whose correct canonical zones are already identified in zone hints but have not been pinned. The cumulative effect is that automated zone health dashboards carry at least 13 structurally invalid file-to-zone assignments, producing unreliable per-zone metrics for web-landing-scripts, web-static-assets, and crash-recovery simultaneously.

The monorepo has no zone that serves as a canonical home for repository-wide tooling scripts (scripts/). The two analysis scripts currently stranded in web-landing-scripts would naturally belong there, but the project-root zone (31 files, cohesion 1) is the closest candidate. Without an explicit tooling zone, repo-wide scripts will continue to fall into residual zones on each analysis run.

crash-recovery's runtime circular dependency with web-dashboard is the only unresolved runtime cycle in the entire monorepo. All other bidirectional coupling edges are either type-only (erased at compile time) or mediated by gateways. This makes crash-recovery the single exception to the monorepo's otherwise cycle-free runtime dependency graph — a status that should be explicitly documented and enforced via a lint rule rather than left to zone hint prose.

The monorepo has exactly two cohesion-1 zones with non-trivial file counts: e2e-tests (22 files) and hench (155 files). Both achieve perfect cohesion with zero coupling. This pattern confirms that the two strongest architectural disciplines in the codebase are (a) test isolation via spawn-only CLI interaction and (b) the gateway pattern — the same mechanism produces the same metric outcome at different scales.

web-unit and logo both exhibit zero cohesion and zero coupling, matching the resolved residual zones (web-landing-scripts, web-static-assets). Three previously dissolved residual zones shared this profile; the presence of two unresolved zones with identical signatures indicates the zone-pin resolution pass was incomplete and a second round of pinning is needed.

The crash zone (5 files, cohesion 0.29) and web-server zone (4 files) are the two smallest non-artifact zones and both have non-zero coupling. The minimum viable zone size for producing meaningful cohesion metrics appears to be approximately 5 files — zones smaller than this behave like interface declarations and should be evaluated on coupling density per file rather than cohesion score.

Across all zones in the graph, every zone with non-zero coupling is contained within the web package. The e2e-tests zone is the only multi-package consumer zone with zero coupling. This bimodal distribution (web = all coupling, everything else = zero) means the web package is the sole ongoing governance burden for import hygiene in the entire monorepo.

The root zone (31 files, cohesion 1) and hench zone (155 files, cohesion 1) together span the orchestration and execution tiers respectively with perfect cohesion. The domain tier (rex, sourcevision) and foundation tier (llm-client) zones are not visible in this batch but if they also achieve high cohesion, the four-tier hierarchy diagram in CLAUDE.md would be directly validated by zone metrics — a testable architectural invariant worth capturing in automated zone health checks.

Zone health metrics (cohesion, coupling) are computed uniformly across all zone types, including artifact-only zones (.rex runtime data, docs) and mixed-concern zones (web-viewer-utilities). Applying thresholds to zones where metrics are structurally meaningless generates false-positive warnings and dilutes the signal-to-noise ratio for genuine architectural violations. Zone type annotations (e.g. 'artifact', 'documentation', 'mixed-pending-pins') with per-type threshold overrides would eliminate structural noise.

The project has two distinct enforcement layers — domain-isolation.test.js for cross-package gateway discipline, and Louvain zone analysis for structural health — but no enforcement layer for intra-zone layering within large zones. Zones with 100+ files (web-viewer at 367, hench-agent at 155) can accumulate internal coupling violations that are invisible until cohesion degrades in a future analysis run. A directory-level import boundary lint rule for large zones would close this gap.

Repository-wide tooling scripts (scripts/hench-callgraph-analysis.mjs, scripts/parse-gateway-exports.mjs) have no designated zone. They fall into residual zones on every analysis run, will receive incorrect zone health thresholds, and cannot be governed by zone-specific pin rules. A 'project-tooling' or 'project-root' zone should be designated to absorb them permanently.

The monorepo's only runtime import cycle (crash-recovery ↔ web-dashboard, 3 imports each direction) is documented in zone hints but not enforced by any automated check. The domain-isolation.test.js that guards the gateway pattern has no equivalent test preventing new runtime cycles from being introduced. A cycle-detection test at the module graph level would prevent this exception from silently expanding.

web-unit and logo zones remain unresolved with zero cohesion and zero coupling — the same profile as the three previously dissolved residual zones. The zone-pin resolution pass is incomplete; these two zones should be dissolved in the same maintenance action that resolved web-landing-scripts and web-static-assets.

No zone in the visible graph represents sourcevision or rex domain packages directly — their internal zone decomposition is absent from this batch. If rex and sourcevision each resolve to a single monolithic zone at high cohesion, the zone graph provides no granularity for detecting intra-package coupling degradation as those packages grow, unlike hench which is explicitly modeled as a 155-file zone.

Zone IDs use two inconsistent naming conventions across the monorepo: full compound nouns (rex-runtime-data, web-viewer-utilities, hench-agent, architecture-docs) and single-word abbreviations (crash, e2e, logo). Normalizing to full compound nouns (crash-recovery, e2e-tests, logo-assets) would make zone IDs self-documenting in automated tooling output and eliminate ambiguity when zone IDs are used as keys in config or reports.

The web package's two gateways (rex-gateway.ts in web-dashboard, domain-gateway.ts in mcp-route-layer) reside in different zones. A cross-package gateway audit that scans one zone produces an incomplete picture of the web package's full cross-package import surface. This is not documented in zone hints for either zone, so automated audits have no signal that two zones must be scanned together to get a complete gateway inventory.

web-viewer is the only zone in the monorepo with three simultaneous bidirectional coupling partners, yet it has no barrel export or explicit API surface — the inverse of hench-agent, which achieves zero external coupling precisely by gating all cross-package access through a single explicit gateway. This asymmetry means the two largest zones in the codebase have opposite encapsulation disciplines, and the larger, higher-risk one (web-viewer) is the less disciplined of the two.

Across the zone graph, the 'crash' zone ID is the only zone whose ID does not match the name used to describe it in prose and zone hints ('crash-recovery'). ID-name mismatches in zone metadata cause automated reporting tools to display different names than developers recognize from documentation, increasing the cognitive load of correlating tool output to architecture docs.

Three of five web-package zones use the 'web-' name prefix (web-dashboard, web-landing-scripts, web-static-assets) while two do not (mcp-route-layer, crash-recovery). This inconsistency means no single name-pattern filter can enumerate all web-package zones — tooling must maintain an explicit zone-ID allowlist rather than relying on prefix matching, and any new web-package zone has no clear naming convention to follow.

crash-detector.ts (browser platform layer, zero framework imports) and use-crash-recovery.ts (Preact hook, framework-coupled) represent two distinct abstraction layers — platform and framework — that happen to share a thematic concern. Their co-location in a single zone creates a hidden coupling risk: any new file added to crash-recovery that imports from both files simultaneously would silently bridge the platform/framework boundary without triggering a gateway or isolation check.

landing.ts in packages/web/src/landing/ is the only production file in the web package that is explicitly documented as 'Vanilla JS (no framework dependency)' — yet it currently resides in a residual zone (web-landing-scripts) rather than being classified as an [entrypoint] alongside the other web viewer entry surfaces. Framework-free page scripts and framework-coupled viewer hooks should be distinguished by archetype to ensure zone analysis correctly identifies their different portability and dependency profiles.

The test zone quality metric is bimodal across the monorepo: zones with clear architectural discipline achieve cohesion 1.0 with zero coupling (e2e-tests, hench), while zones without it collapse to zero cohesion (web-unit, logo) — this bimodal distribution suggests test zone health is a direct proxy for architectural clarity in the corresponding production zone

The e2e-tests zone is the only zone that cross-cuts all four tiers of the dependency hierarchy (orchestration → execution → domain → foundation) via spawned processes, yet no automated check verifies that all four tiers remain exercisable through the CLI surface — a tier-reachability test would complement the existing architecture-policy.test.js gate

Across the monorepo, the two zones with 100% cohesion and zero coupling at non-trivial scale (e2e-tests at 22 files, hench at 155 files) both achieve this through the same mechanism: strict spawn-only or gateway-only interaction with external surfaces. This is a testable architectural invariant — any zone that reaches cohesion 1.0 with zero coupling does so by enforcing a single interaction boundary pattern

The web package is the only package with test files distributed across multiple zones (e2e-tests for orchestration contracts, web-unit for package unit tests) with no zone connecting them — unlike rex, sourcevision, and hench where unit and integration tests are colocated within a single coherent package zone, the web package's test topology is fragmented and governed by different discipline levels simultaneously

The absence of a dedicated cli-mcp.test.js creates an asymmetric coverage gap: ndx start is tested for CLI argument contracts but not for the HTTP service it starts, meaning the most stateful command in the orchestration tier (it runs a persistent server) has less coverage depth than stateless commands like ndx status or ndx usage

Standardize zone IDs to full compound nouns: rename 'crash' → 'crash-recovery', 'e2e' → 'e2e-tests', 'logo' → 'logo-assets'. All other multi-word zones already follow this convention; these three abbreviations are the only exceptions and cause ID-name mismatches in documentation references.

Document in zone hints for both web-dashboard and mcp-route-layer that the web package has two gateways in two separate zones. Cross-package gateway audits currently have no machine-readable signal that both zones must be scanned to obtain a complete import surface inventory, making partial audits appear complete.

web-viewer participates in three simultaneous bidirectional import cycles with no barrel export controlling its public API surface, while hench-agent achieves zero external coupling through an explicit gateway. Apply barrel-export discipline to web-viewer (src/viewer/index.ts) to align the web package's largest zone with the encapsulation pattern proven effective at the 155-file scale in hench-agent.

Establish and document a 'web-' naming prefix requirement for all zones whose files reside primarily within the packages/web/ directory. Currently three zones comply and two do not; enforcing this in zone hints or a lint rule would make package ownership discoverable from zone IDs alone and prevent future zones from silently omitting the prefix.

crash-detector.ts is a framework-agnostic browser platform utility currently classified as [utility] and co-located with a Preact hook in the crash-recovery zone. Consider introducing a 'web-platform-utilities' zone or archetype for framework-free browser API wrappers (sessionStorage, performance APIs) to create an explicit separation between platform-layer code and framework-layer hooks — the same distinction that separates server infrastructure from viewer components in web-dashboard.

The .js vs .ts test file extension split (e2e-tests zone uses .js, all domain package test zones use .ts) should be resolved by converting e2e tests to TypeScript — this gap means the orchestration tier's integration contracts are type-checked less rigorously than domain-tier unit tests, which inverts the expected risk-to-rigor relationship

No automated check prevents new bidirectional import cycles from being added to web-viewer, which already participates in three simultaneous bidirectional cycles (web-dashboard, web-server, crash) — a coupling-cap assertion in domain-isolation.test.js that fails if web-viewer's bidirectional partner count exceeds three would enforce the documented intentional-coupling surface as a hard constraint

The architecture-policy.test.js gate lives in the slow e2e suite (spawns processes) but enforces rules (import boundaries, gateway discipline) that can be checked statically — moving policy enforcement to a dedicated fast lint-time zone (e.g. policy-enforcement) would allow CI to fail on architectural violations in seconds rather than minutes

The crash-recovery zone exhibits the 'forced satellite' anti-pattern: two files that don't directly import each other are grouped together because they share a thematic concern (crash handling) rather than an import relationship. The low cohesion (0.29) is a direct consequence. If this zone were dissolved and both files pinned to web-dashboard, the cohesion metric would disappear but architectural clarity would improve.

e2e-tests zone covers 22 orchestration CLI contracts but domain packages (rex, sourcevision, hench) have no peer e2e zone visible in the zone graph — if domain CLIs are exercised only through unit tests, regressions in their public CLI surface may go undetected until the orchestration layer fails.
Star topology via rex-gateway.ts is the reference pattern for safe cross-package coupling — one explicit choke point vs the web-viewer hub's many-to-many crossing imports.

Anti-corruption layer pattern: mcp-route-layer isolates the MCP protocol surface from rex and sourcevision domain internals via the gateway module. This prevents protocol-level concerns (session IDs, HTTP framing) from leaking into domain logic and vice versa. The pattern is correctly implemented and should be the reference for any future protocol adapter zones.

Hub-and-spoke topology: web-dashboard is the composition root that all peer zones import into. Its near-zero outbound coupling (0.02) combined with being the target of every other zone's coupling edges confirms it holds the wiring logic for the entire package. This is intentional but means changes to web-dashboard's public surface ripple across all satellite zones.

This zone is a Louvain residual with no architectural coherence. The three file categories it contains (production, test, tooling) each have distinct owners and should be resolved independently: landing.ts → web-dashboard, integration tests → e2e or web-dashboard test suite, root scripts → project-root zone. Until pins are applied, any zone-level health metrics for this zone are meaningless.

Cross-package residual zone in violation of the documented zone prohibition. PNG logo assets and index.html belong to web-dashboard (viewer static files); tests/integration/ files belong to the project-root or e2e zone. This is the highest-priority zone pin action because cross-package groupings produce unreliable coupling and cohesion metrics that could mask real architectural issues.

crash-recovery ↔ web-dashboard bidirectional coupling (3 imports each direction) creates a small import cycle. Unlike the mcp-route-layer, which has a type-only reverse edge, crash-recovery's reverse imports are runtime (use-crash-recovery.ts hooks back into viewer state). This makes crash-recovery the only zone in this batch with a true runtime circular dependency with web-dashboard, even if intentional.

mcp-route-layer → web-dashboard coupling (0.6) flows in one direction only at the zone level: routes-mcp.ts is imported by web-dashboard's start.ts composition root. The reverse edge (web-dashboard → mcp-route-layer) exists only as a type-import (erased at compile time). This asymmetry is healthy — the ACL is consumed by the composition root, not entangled with it.

web-dashboard absorbs inbound coupling from three satellite zones (crash-recovery, mcp-route-layer, and residual zones) while keeping its own outbound coupling at 0.02 — an asymmetric hub pattern. If web-dashboard's internal cohesion degrades in future runs, this asymmetry will make it harder to split because all satellites would need re-wiring simultaneously.
All 13 outbound coupling imports target web-dashboard; there are no cross-package imports, confirming the coupling metric is a zone classification artifact rather than an architectural defect.

CLAUDE.md contains an explicit sync notice requiring CODEX.md to mirror several sections, but no automated check enforces this invariant. In a monorepo with multiple active contributors, documentation drift between CLAUDE.md and CODEX.md is likely to go undetected. A CI lint step comparing the two files' mirrored sections would make this a hard failure rather than a manual audit responsibility.

The runtime bidirectional dependency between crash-recovery and web-dashboard (3 imports each direction) has no interface boundary — crash-recovery directly imports from web-dashboard viewer state and web-dashboard directly imports crash-detector. If crash handling ever needs to support server-side error boundaries (process signals, middleware), the viewer-coupled implementation cannot be reused without extracting a shared interface, and the coupling surface would have to expand into a new zone simultaneously.

No e2e test file targets the 'ndx init' multi-package initialization chain — cli-contract.test.js and cli-delegation.test.js cover delegation but initialization sequencing (sourcevision init → rex init → hench init) has no dedicated coverage, leaving cross-package init ordering regressions undetected.

The e2e suite covers orchestration commands exhaustively but hench's autonomous agent loop (ndx work) is tested only through cli-arg-contracts — no e2e test validates end-to-end task selection, brief construction, and LLM dispatch, meaning the highest-value user path has the weakest integration coverage.

No intra-zone submodule boundary enforcement exists between agent/analysis/, store/, and prd/ subdirectories. The domain-isolation test only enforces cross-package gateway discipline; intra-package layering violations (e.g. agent importing directly from prd/ internals) are undetected. A zone-internal layer test or directory-level import lint rule would catch coupling decay before it affects cohesion scores.

The MCP route layer's two domain gateways are split across two zones: domain-gateway.ts (sourcevision) lives in mcp-route-layer while rex-gateway.ts (rex) lives in web-dashboard. A developer auditing cross-package MCP imports must inspect both zones to see the full surface, weakening the gateway pattern's auditability guarantee.

Execution log rotation policy is undocumented. CLAUDE.md documents that archive.json is capped at 100 batches, but execution-log.jsonl has no stated bound. Without a documented policy, operators have no signal for when to intervene and automated tooling cannot enforce limits.

rex-gateway.ts (the cross-package gateway for rex imports) resides in web-dashboard rather than in the mcp-route-layer zone where domain-gateway.ts (the sourcevision gateway) lives. The one-gateway-per-source-package rule is satisfied in isolation, but the two gateways serving the same consumer (MCP routes) are in different zones, violating the spirit of the auditable import surface the gateway pattern is meant to provide.

Zone name 'web-landing-scripts' is semantically incorrect for 6 of its 8 files: two root-level analysis scripts (scripts/), two web package integration contract tests, and two viewer unit tests have no relationship to landing-page scripting.
